explanation: The requirement 'Any prop_0 shall leave the prop_1' can be understood as: whenever prop_0 and prop_1 are true together (i.e., prop_0 is 'in' prop_1), eventually, prop_0 will not be true with prop_1 (it will leave). In LTL, this is usually written as: Whenever (prop_0 & prop_1) holds, then eventually (!prop_0 | !prop_1) will hold.
The statement means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same moment. This is best captured by 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

The statement says that whenever prop_0 holds, after some time prop_1 will no longer hold (i.e., 'leave' means prop_1 becomes false at some point after prop_0 is true). For every moment where prop_0 is true, at some future point, prop_1 becomes false. This corresponds to: 'Globally, if prop_0 is true, then eventually not prop_1.'

'Any prop_0 shall leave the prop_1' can be interpreted as: whenever prop_0 is true, at some future point prop_1 must become false. This can be written as: Globally, if prop_0 is true, then eventually (in the future) prop_1 is not true.

The statement says that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' must eventually become false (i.e., 'prop_0' triggers 'not prop_1'). In LTL, this is captured by: globally, if prop_0 holds, then eventually not prop_1 holds.

This sentence means that, whenever prop_0 holds, it must eventually become true that prop_1 does not hold (i.e., prop_0 will eventually leave the set of states where prop_1 is true). In LTL, for any state where prop_0 is true, in the future there exists a state where prop_0 is true and prop_1 is false, and until then, prop_0 continues to hold. This is captured by: G(prop_0 -> F(!prop_1)).

Whenever prop_0 occurs, in the resulting state it must still be possible for both prop_1 and prop_2 to eventually occur. In LTL, this is formalized as: whenever prop_0 happens, in the next state, both 'eventually prop_1' and 'eventually prop_2' hold.

This statement suggests that whenever prop_0 holds, it should eventually become false, meaning the system must guarantee that prop_0 does not stay true forever. This is captured by the LTL formula: G (prop_0 -> F (!prop_0)). The mention of prop_1 is not directly related unless there is further context.

The requirement 'Any prop_0 shall leave the prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 and prop_1 are both true, eventually prop_1 must become false (i.e., prop_0 leaves prop_1). This can be expressed as: 'Globally, if prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then eventually not prop_1 holds.'

The sentence implies that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should not hold afterwards, or prop_0 can only occur when prop_1 does not occur. In LTL, this can be expressed by stating that "globally, if prop_0 occurs, then globally after that, prop_1 does not hold". This is captured as G(prop_0 -> G(!prop_1)).
